questions.question_text: When must a Delaware licensee provide the Consumer Information Statement to a prospective buyer?
questions.correct_answer: At the earliest of the first appointment, first showing, or offer
distractors: At the time of contract signing
questions.explanation: Under 24 Del. C. §2938(b), Delaware licensees must provide the CIS at the earliest of three statutory triggers: the first scheduled appointment, the first showing of a property, or the making of an offer. (The phrase 'first substantive contact' belongs to §2932(c), which governs disclosure by licensees acting under common-law agency — the CIS itself runs on the three listed triggers.)

questions.question_text: A licensee shows a buyer three homes but never provides the Consumer Information Statement. What violation occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Failure to provide CIS before sharing info
distractors: No violation if buyer didn't ask
Violation only if contract is signed
questions.explanation: Under 24 Del. C. §2938, the CIS is due at the earliest of the first scheduled appointment, the first showing of a property, or making an offer. By showing three homes without providing it, the licensee blew past the first-showing trigger on the very first property and was in violation from that point on.

questions.question_text: What does the Consumer Information Statement primarily explain to consumers?
questions.explanation: The CIS explains the different types of agency relationships available, the duties owed under each, and the consumer's right to choose their representation. It is an educational document designed to help consumers make informed decisions.

questions.question_text: A buyer refuses to sign the CIS acknowledgment but wants to continue working with the licensee. What should the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Note the refusal and continue service
distractors: Refuse to work with the buyer at all
Have the broker sign on buyer's behalf
Skip the CIS requirement for this buyer
questions.explanation: Delaware law does not bar a licensee from continuing to work with a consumer who declines to sign; 24 Del. C. §2938(b) requires the signature only before the consumer signs an agreement of sale, listing agreement, or other brokerage agreement. Best practice is to note the refusal and the delivery date — the licensee signs and dates the CIS confirming when it was given. No agreement of sale or brokerage agreement should be signed until the CIS is signed.

questions.question_text: The CIS is required in which type of Delaware real estate transaction?
questions.correct_answer: Sales and rentals of 1-to-4 family residential property
questions.explanation: The CIS is required for sales of 1-to-4 family residential properties (and single lots intended for one) and for residential rentals (24 Del. C. §2938). Exempt: nonrenewable leases of 120 days or less (§2938(e)) and non-1-to-4-family transactions such as commercial deals (§2938(f)), where the agreement of sale instead contains statutory acknowledgment language. There is no price threshold.

questions.question_text: Without a written agency agreement, a Delaware licensee working with a buyer is presumed to be what?
questions.explanation: Under 24 Del. C. §2933(c)(1), a licensee working with a buyer on a 1-to-4 family residential transaction is presumed to be a statutory agent representing the buyer. A statutory agent owes the statutory duties in §2936 — not common-law fiduciary duties; the licensee acts as an independent contractor, not a fiduciary. The presumption can be rebutted by signing a CIS establishing a different relationship (§2933(c)(3)).

questions.question_text: A Delaware licensee finds themselves working with both the buyer and the seller in the same 1-to-4 family residential transaction. Under Delaware law, how does dual agency arise?
questions.correct_answer: It is presumed by statute unless a party opts out on the CIS
distractors: Only with written informed consent signed by both parties
Only with prior approval from the Real Estate Commission
It is prohibited in residential transactions
questions.explanation: Under 24 Del. C. §2933(c)(1), a licensee working for both buyer and seller is presumed to be a statutory agent representing both parties as a dual agent. No separate written consent is required — consumers are informed through the Consumer Information Statement and may rebut the presumption by electing a different relationship on the CIS (§2933(c)(3)). A dual agent still may not disclose either party's confidential information (§2936(c)).

questions.question_text: A dual agent learns the buyer will pay $20,000 over asking price. Can the agent share this with the seller?
distractors: Yes, the seller is also a client
Yes, if the broker approves it
Only after the offer is submitted
questions.explanation: A dual agent cannot disclose either party's confidential information to the other. A buyer's willingness to pay more than offered is confidential. Sharing it would breach the duty owed to the buyer under the dual agency arrangement.

questions.question_text: A licensee is a presumed dual agent representing both buyer and seller under Delaware's statutory agency law. Which duty is most limited in dual agency?
questions.explanation: Under dual agency, the agent's attention is divided between two parties with potentially competing interests. Delaware dual agency arises by statutory presumption (24 Del. C. §2933(c)(1)); the dual agent cannot advocate exclusively for either side and may not share either party's confidential information (§2936(c)). Honesty, accounting, and material-fact disclosure still apply fully.

questions.question_text: A buyer walks into an open house without a buyer's agent. Under Delaware law, what is the hosting agent's role?
questions.correct_answer: The seller's agent with seller duties
distractors: A dual agent for both parties now
A neutral facilitator for all parties
questions.explanation: The open house agent already has an agency relationship with the seller. The unrepresented buyer is a customer, not a client. Presumed buyer agency applies only when a licensee begins working with a buyer, not at a casual open house visit.

questions.question_text: Which duty does a Delaware licensee owe to ALL parties in a transaction, not just clients?
questions.correct_answer: Honesty and disclosure of material facts
distractors: Advocacy for the party's price objectives
questions.explanation: Delaware statutory agents owe honesty, reasonable skill and care, timely accounting, and disclosure of adverse material facts actually known (24 Del. C. §2936(b)) in dealings with all parties. Unusually, Delaware also extends confidentiality to customers from first contact (§§2934(a), 2936(c)). Exclusive advocacy is not owed to non-clients.

questions.question_text: A listing agent's client relationship ends. The seller later asks a new agent about the former client's motivation to sell. Must the original agent's confidentiality continue?
distractors: Only if a written NDA was signed
questions.explanation: In Delaware, the duty of confidentiality survives the termination of the agency relationship. A former agent must never disclose confidential client information such as motivation to sell, even after the relationship has ended.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered confidential client information in Delaware?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer's willingness to pay more
questions.explanation: A buyer's willingness to pay more than offered is confidential and must not be disclosed. Material property defects, zoning status, and required disclosures are not confidential — they must be shared with all parties.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent discovers the client is financially unable to close. Which duty requires informing the seller's side?
questions.correct_answer: Disclosure of material facts to all parties
questions.explanation: A buyer's inability to perform is material to the transaction. While Delaware licensees generally keep parties' information confidential, 24 Del. C. §2936(c)(5) removes protection where failing to disclose would constitute fraud or intentional misrepresentation, and §2929 forbids passing on financial information known to be false. Continuing to present the buyer as able to close would cross that line, so the material fact must be revealed.

questions.question_text: A client instructs their agent to hide a known drainage problem from buyers. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Refuse and disclose the material defect
distractors: Ask the broker for a confidentiality waiver
questions.explanation: A licensee must not follow unlawful instructions. Concealing a known material defect violates the licensee's statutory duty to disclose adverse material facts actually known (24 Del. C. §2936(b)(8)) and the seller's own disclosure duty (6 Del. C. §2572); §2936(c)(5) provides that confidentiality never shields information whose concealment would constitute fraud. The agent must refuse and ensure proper disclosure.
